Common Mistakes When Promoting
Common Mistakes Managers Make
1. Lack of separation: You need to separate the person’s new job from their old job.
In that transition time, if you let the overlap continue too long, then the person is
doing both jobs. This way there is no opportunity for the employee to take on the
new role to the best of their ability.
2. Training Deficit: The current teammate/supervisor feels that he or she can train
the new person, but then becomes busy with other business demands, so then
the new person doesn’t get a full set of training.
3. Keeping it a Secret: Announce the position in a big way. When you promote
internally, you don’t have this brand new personality that’s joining the staff. Do a
shoutout on the daily huddle, send an email to the team, let the clients know!
4. Time to Hire: Don’t hire internally out of desperation. It happens every time in
every business and in is a short term band aid that does more harm than good.
5. People-Pleasing: a lot of managers make the mistake of trying to appease
longtime workers who seem dissatisfied by offering them either a transfer or a
promotion within the company that is not in the company or teams best interest.
Common Mistakes Teammates Make
1. Asking for too much at once: many ask for a promotion, raise & more freedom
at the same time. This will frustrate your supervisor.
2. Overshooting Your Target: many employees make the mistake of asking for a
promotion that they don’t yet have the skills yet to earn. If you are not sure if you
have the skillset, have a discussion with your supervisor first before bulldozing
your way to the interview.
3. Choosing Status over Fulfillment: this is the most common mistake. Many
employees think they want to move up, but do not understand what the role
actually entails. They lack the objectivity to look at their strengths vs the
strengths required for the role as well as the job vs what they want as a job. The
idea of more authority, pay & status can blind people from seeing themselves and
their wants/needs objectively.
4. Not telling: Nobody can read your mind, if you don’t voice that you want a
promotion, don’t expect one. Introverts are the ones who fall easily into this trap
and really need to step out of their comfort zones.
5. Stepping on Others: Of course it is obvious that most promotions are due to an
increase in responsibility because someone has done one job with excellence,
however - it doesn’t matter how great of results you produce if people don’t trust
you or have a hard time working with you. Oftentimes, people will expect a
promotion due to their results individually rather than their contribution to the
team.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
